title: In-Ear EEG Biometrics for Feasible and Readily Collectable Real-World Person Authentication
venue: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security

method.input_representation: Autoregressive (AR) coefficients and Power Spectral Density (PSD) features (specifically alpha band ratios and peak power)
method.training_strategy: 5-fold cross-validation within the training matrix for SVM hyper-parameter tuning

evaluation.context: Achieved in Setup-B (biased) using SVM for registered imposters (derived from TPR=99.7%)
Achieved in Setup-B (biased) using SVM for clients (derived from TPR=89.3%)

evaluation_protocol: Compared two scenarios: Setup-R (Rigorous, training and validation from different days) and Setup-B (Biased, training and validation mix data from the same day)

reported_limitations: Sensitivity to drowsiness/fatigue causing alpha attenuation
Performance drop when training/testing are on different days

usability.usability_issues: Requires the user to be in a resting state with eyes closed

key_contribution: Demonstrated feasibility of in-ear EEG for biometric authentication
Introduced a 'rigorous' evaluation protocol to avoid data leakage between recording days
main_strength: High collectability via wearable in-ear sensor
Experimental focus on temporal reproducibility across different days
main_weakness: Data collected exclusively in a controlled lab setting
Dependence on specific mental state (eyes closed)
insights.notes_for_review: The authors highlight a significant performance gap between biased (same-day) and rigorous (cross-day) setups, suggesting that many existing EEG biometric studies overestimate performance.
